text.title: Diary Study Execution
text.summary: Monitoring participant compliance, detecting low-quality entries, managing drop-off, and maintaining data quality during an active diary study.

section.title: Monitoring

rule.statement: Monitor compliance daily and flag participants below 60% completion by day 4. Do not wait until study end to detect dropout.
rule.rationale: Early dropout is recoverable with a human check-in call. Late detection produces data gaps that make cross-participant comparison unreliable.

list.item: Check completion rates daily per participant.
Send reminders at the scheduled cadence — switch from automated nudges to a single human-style DM after two missed days.
Monitor data quality: look for generic entries, copy-paste repetition, off-topic content.

section.reference: Bash script flagging low-completion participants and suspected copy-paste entries.

rule.statement: Compute text similarity across a participant's entries. Flag entries with cosine similarity above 0.9 as suspected copy-paste before including them in analysis.
rule.rationale: Copy-paste entries inflate frequency counts for the copied text and suppress the genuine signal from days the participant did not engage. Counting them as real entries poisons temporal pattern analysis.

list.item: Generic entries (same text, low detail): prompt participant for specifics in a follow-up message.
Missed days (gap in submissions): send gentle reminder within 24 hours.
Copy-paste entries (identical across days): schedule a recovery check-in call.
Off-topic content: clarify study scope — never silently discard without documenting.

rule.statement: Mitigate self-report bias by using signal-contingent prompts and keeping study duration under 21 days. Acknowledge remaining bias explicitly in the final report.
rule.rationale: Social desirability, memory reconstruction, observation effect, and fatigue all systematically distort self-report data. Transparency about remaining bias prevents stakeholders from over-interpreting findings.

list.item: Social desirability: participants report what seems "right", not what actually happened.
Memory reconstruction: entries written hours after events reconstruct rather than recall.
Observation effect: behavior changes when participants know they are being studied.
Fatigue effects: entry quality and detail decline over the second half of long studies.

rule.statement: Pay incentives in tranches (30% at week 1 completion, 70% at study end). Pre-paid full incentives correlate with mid-study dropout.
rule.rationale: Tranche payment keeps commitment active through the critical second week when fatigue peaks. Pre-payment removes the ongoing motivation to continue.

rule.statement: Send a personal check-in call (15 minutes) at the midpoint of the study. This single intervention lifts completion rates measurably.
rule.rationale: Check-in calls rebuild commitment and catch confusion about entry expectations before the participant fully disengages. Automated reminders alone cannot substitute for this human contact.
